requirements.requirement: Deploy multi-layer access controls progressing from perimeter (fencing, barriers) to building (card access), floor (card + PIN), and server room (card + biometric) with all access events logged centrally
requirements.rationale: Defence-in-depth physical security ensures that compromise of one layer does not grant access to critical infrastructure; BNM expects progressive security zones

requirements.requirement: Install CCTV surveillance covering all entry/exit points, corridors, server rooms, and sensitive areas with minimum 90-day footage retention, tamper-evident recording, and adequate resolution for identification purposes
requirements.rationale: BNM specifically mandates 90-day CCTV retention; inadequate coverage or retention periods are a common and easily verifiable examination finding

requirements.requirement: Integrate physical access control, CCTV, and intrusion detection systems into a centralised security monitoring platform with real-time alerting and incident response procedures
requirements.rationale: Siloed security systems delay detection and response; integrated monitoring provides correlation of physical security events and faster incident resolution

governance.summary: Governance must enforce formal physical security policies, maintain auditable access logs and visitor records, and ensure regular independent assessments of data centre physical security controls.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal data centre physical security policy approved by senior management, covering access control requirements, CCTV standards, visitor management, and security incident response
requirements.rationale: A board-approved policy demonstrates governance commitment to physical security and provides the framework for consistent implementation across all facilities

requirements.requirement: Review physical access rights quarterly, removing access for terminated employees within 24 hours and for role-changed employees within 48 hours, with documented evidence of each review cycle
requirements.rationale: Stale access rights are a frequent examination finding; quarterly reviews with immediate revocation upon separation demonstrate active access governance

requirements.requirement: Commission annual independent physical security assessments covering all data centre facilities, with findings tracked to remediation and reported to the IT Risk Committee
requirements.rationale: Independent assessments provide objective validation of physical security controls and identify blind spots that internal reviews may overlook

business.summary: Environmental failures in data centres -- such as overheating, humidity damage, or water leaks -- can cause hardware failures and extended outages that disrupt critical banking services and result in significant financial losses.

requirements.requirement: Invest in enterprise-grade environmental monitoring systems with redundant sensors, automated alerting, and integration with building management systems to protect the institution's technology infrastructure
requirements.rationale: Environmental incidents are a leading cause of unplanned data centre outages; proactive monitoring and early warning systems significantly reduce the risk and impact of environmental failures

requirements.requirement: Ensure environmental control systems (HVAC, water leak detection, air quality management) are covered under maintenance contracts with defined response times aligned to the criticality of the data centre
requirements.rationale: Equipment failures without maintenance coverage lead to extended resolution times, increasing the risk of cascading failures and prolonged service disruptions

requirements.requirement: Include environmental control failure scenarios in business impact analysis and ensure adequate insurance coverage for equipment damage from environmental incidents
requirements.rationale: Understanding the financial impact of environmental failures enables appropriate investment in controls and insurance, reducing the institution's exposure to catastrophic losses

technology.summary: Environmental controls must include continuous monitoring of temperature (18-27 degrees Celsius), humidity (40-60% RH), water leak detection beneath raised floors and near cooling units, and air quality management, all with automated threshold-based alerting.

requirements.requirement: Deploy temperature and humidity sensors at multiple points within each server room (intake, exhaust, hot aisle, cold aisle) with continuous monitoring and automated alerts when readings breach defined thresholds (temperature: 18-27 degrees Celsius, humidity: 40-60% relative humidity)
requirements.rationale: Single-point monitoring misses localised hotspots; distributed sensors with ASHRAE-aligned thresholds ensure comprehensive coverage and early detection of environmental anomalies

requirements.requirement: Install water leak detection systems beneath raised floors, near cooling units, and along water supply routes, with automated alerting and integration into the building management system (BMS)
requirements.rationale: Water leaks can cause catastrophic equipment damage in minutes; early detection through distributed sensors minimises the blast radius of water-related incidents

requirements.requirement: Configure automated escalation procedures for environmental alerts with tiered response levels: warning (email notification), critical (SMS and email to on-call team), and emergency (automated shutdown of affected zones to prevent equipment damage)
requirements.rationale: Alerts without defined escalation paths lead to delayed response; tiered escalation ensures the right personnel are engaged at the right time based on severity

requirements.requirement: Maintain redundant HVAC systems with N+1 cooling capacity to ensure continued environmental control during maintenance or failure of individual cooling units
requirements.rationale: Single-point HVAC failure without redundancy leads to rapid temperature rise and potential equipment shutdown; N+1 redundancy ensures continuity during maintenance windows

governance.summary: Governance must ensure environmental monitoring procedures are documented, threshold settings are formally approved, monitoring data is retained for analysis, and environmental incidents are tracked and reported.

requirements.requirement: Document and formally approve environmental monitoring procedures including sensor placement standards, threshold settings, alert escalation paths, and response procedures for each type of environmental event
requirements.rationale: Documented procedures with approved thresholds demonstrate to BNM examiners that environmental management is systematic rather than ad hoc

requirements.requirement: Generate and review monthly environmental monitoring reports covering temperature and humidity trends, threshold breaches, alert response times, and corrective actions taken
requirements.rationale: Trend analysis from regular reporting identifies deteriorating conditions before they cause incidents and demonstrates proactive environmental management to auditors

requirements.requirement: Track all environmental incidents to root cause resolution with post-incident reviews, and report significant environmental events to the IT Risk Committee as part of operational risk reporting
requirements.rationale: Incident tracking and root cause analysis prevent recurrence and provide management visibility into the reliability of data centre environmental controls

business.summary: Power failures are the single most common cause of data centre outages; without redundant power supplies and backup generators capable of sustaining 72-hour operations, the institution risks extended service disruptions affecting all banking channels.

requirements.requirement: Invest in redundant power infrastructure including dual utility feeds, UPS systems, and backup generators with minimum 72-hour fuel capacity for all critical data centre facilities
requirements.rationale: BNM mandates 72-hour generator runtime; insufficient power redundancy is a critical finding that can result in regulatory action and indicates inadequate resilience planning

requirements.requirement: Establish fuel supply agreements with at least two independent suppliers to ensure continuous generator operation beyond the initial 72-hour reserve during extended power outages
requirements.rationale: Extended outages exceeding initial fuel reserves are possible during natural disasters; diverse fuel supply agreements ensure sustained generator operations

requirements.requirement: Include power failure scenarios in business continuity planning with defined load-shedding priorities to ensure the most critical banking services are maintained during degraded power conditions
requirements.rationale: Not all services can be sustained indefinitely on backup power; predefined load-shedding priorities ensure that customer-critical services are prioritised during power constraints

technology.summary: Power redundancy architecture must include dual utility feeds (where available), N+1 UPS systems with adequate battery runtime for generator start-up, and diesel generators with 72-hour fuel reserves, all subject to regular failover testing.

requirements.requirement: Design and maintain power distribution with 2N or N+1 redundancy including dual power feeds to critical racks, redundant UPS systems with minimum 15-minute battery runtime, and automatic transfer switches (ATS) for seamless failover
requirements.rationale: Single points of failure in power distribution can cause localised outages even when utility power is available; redundant distribution ensures resilience against component failures

requirements.requirement: Install and maintain backup generators with rated capacity sufficient to power all critical loads plus 20% headroom, with fuel reserves for minimum 72 continuous hours of operation and automated start-up within 10 seconds of utility power failure
requirements.rationale: BNM explicitly requires 72-hour generator capability; insufficient capacity or fuel reserves is a critical compliance gap that demonstrates inadequate resilience

requirements.requirement: Conduct power failover tests at least quarterly including full load transfer from utility to UPS to generator and back, documenting transfer times, voltage stability, and any anomalies
requirements.rationale: Untested failover mechanisms may fail when needed most; regular testing under load validates that the power redundancy architecture functions as designed

requirements.requirement: Implement continuous power monitoring across all distribution paths with automated alerting for load imbalances, capacity thresholds, UPS battery health degradation, and fuel level warnings
requirements.rationale: Proactive power monitoring identifies developing issues before they cause outages; continuous visibility into power metrics enables predictive maintenance

governance.summary: Governance must ensure power redundancy designs are formally documented and approved, failover tests are scheduled and results reviewed, and power capacity is regularly assessed against current and projected demand.

requirements.requirement: Document the power redundancy architecture including single-line diagrams, capacity calculations, failover sequences, and fuel supply arrangements, with formal approval from the CTO
requirements.rationale: Documented architecture provides the baseline for audits and ensures that all stakeholders understand the power redundancy design and its limitations

requirements.requirement: Review and report power failover test results to the IT Risk Committee, tracking any deficiencies to remediation with defined timelines and responsible owners
requirements.rationale: Management visibility of test results and deficiencies ensures accountability for maintaining power resilience and drives timely remediation of identified gaps

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual power capacity assessments comparing current load against available capacity, projecting growth requirements, and recommending infrastructure upgrades where needed
requirements.rationale: Data centre power demand grows with business expansion; failure to plan for capacity growth can result in overloaded systems and reduced redundancy margins

business.summary: Fire is a catastrophic risk to data centre operations; without appropriate detection and suppression systems, a single fire incident can destroy critical infrastructure, causing prolonged service outages and potentially irrecoverable data loss.

requirements.requirement: Invest in gas-based fire suppression systems (FM-200, Novec 1230, or equivalent) that are safe for electronic equipment and personnel, replacing any water-based sprinkler systems in server rooms
requirements.rationale: Water-based suppression causes as much damage as fire in a data centre; gas-based systems extinguish fires without destroying equipment, significantly reducing recovery time and costs

requirements.requirement: Ensure all data centre staff and security personnel are trained on fire emergency response procedures including evacuation routes, fire suppression system operation, and emergency shutdown procedures
requirements.rationale: Untrained staff may respond incorrectly during a fire, potentially causing injuries, delaying suppression activation, or executing incorrect shutdown sequences that extend recovery time

requirements.requirement: Maintain fire insurance coverage with valuations that reflect current replacement costs of all data centre equipment and estimated business interruption losses
requirements.rationale: Underinsured data centre assets leave the institution exposed to significant unrecoverable financial losses in the event of a major fire incident

technology.summary: Fire protection must include very early smoke detection apparatus (VESDA), gas-based suppression systems appropriate for electronic equipment, fire-resistant construction, and integrated alarm systems with automated emergency response triggers.

requirements.requirement: Install very early smoke detection apparatus (VESDA) or equivalent aspirating smoke detection in all server rooms and critical areas, providing early warning before visible smoke or flame develops
requirements.rationale: Traditional smoke detectors activate too late for data centre environments; VESDA systems detect combustion particles at the earliest stage, enabling response before equipment damage occurs

requirements.requirement: Deploy gas-based fire suppression systems (FM-200, Novec 1230, or inert gas systems such as Inergen) in all server rooms and critical infrastructure areas, with automatic activation linked to detection systems and manual override capability
requirements.rationale: Gas-based suppression protects electronic equipment from water damage while effectively extinguishing fires; automatic activation ensures rapid response even outside staffed hours

requirements.requirement: Test fire detection and suppression systems at least annually in accordance with local fire safety regulations and manufacturer specifications, including detector sensitivity testing, gas cylinder pressure checks, and discharge path verification
requirements.rationale: Untested suppression systems may fail during an actual fire; annual testing validates system readiness and is a specific BNM requirement

requirements.requirement: Integrate fire detection systems with HVAC controls to automatically shut down air handling units upon fire detection, preventing smoke spread through ventilation systems
requirements.rationale: Active HVAC systems spread smoke and fire throughout the facility; automatic HVAC shutdown upon detection contains the fire and smoke to the affected zone

governance.summary: Governance must ensure fire detection and suppression systems are maintained under formal service agreements, testing is scheduled and documented, and emergency response procedures are regularly drilled and updated.

requirements.requirement: Maintain documented fire emergency response procedures covering detection, notification, evacuation, suppression activation, emergency power shutdown, and post-incident recovery, reviewed and updated at least annually
requirements.rationale: Documented procedures ensure consistent and effective response during high-stress fire emergencies; annual review keeps procedures current with any facility or personnel changes

requirements.requirement: Maintain fire suppression system maintenance records, inspection certificates, and annual test results in an auditable format for a minimum of seven years
requirements.rationale: BNM examiners will request evidence of annual testing and ongoing maintenance; incomplete records indicate potential gaps in fire safety compliance

requirements.requirement: Conduct fire drills at least annually for all data centre personnel, documenting participation, observed response times, identified improvements, and follow-up actions
requirements.rationale: Staff training records alone are insufficient; fire drills validate that personnel can execute procedures under simulated emergency conditions and reveal practical gaps

business.summary: Multi-factor physical access controls with biometric authentication are essential to prevent unauthorised access to data centre facilities, protecting the institution's most critical assets from insider threats and physical intrusions.

requirements.requirement: Implement multi-factor physical access controls (card plus biometric) for all data centre facilities, with biometric authentication mandatory for server rooms and sensitive infrastructure areas
requirements.rationale: Card-only access is insufficient for high-security areas as cards can be shared, lost, or cloned; biometric authentication ensures that only the authorised individual gains physical access

requirements.requirement: Ensure HR processes include immediate notification to the data centre access team upon employee termination or role change, with access revocation completed within 24 hours of notification
requirements.rationale: Delayed access revocation after termination creates a window where a disgruntled former employee could physically access and damage critical infrastructure

requirements.requirement: Budget for biometric system maintenance, upgrades, and enrolment processes to ensure reliable operation and prevent degradation of access control effectiveness over time
requirements.rationale: Poorly maintained biometric systems lead to high false-rejection rates, causing staff to bypass controls or prop open doors, undermining the entire access control framework

technology.summary: Multi-factor physical access must combine proximity cards with biometric authentication (fingerprint, iris, or palm vein), integrated with an access control management system that supports real-time monitoring, automated alerts, and quarterly access reviews.

requirements.requirement: Deploy biometric authentication (fingerprint, iris scan, or palm vein) at all entry points to server rooms and sensitive areas, integrated with card-based access at building and floor entry points to provide multi-factor physical authentication
requirements.rationale: BNM explicitly requires biometric authentication for sensitive areas; multi-factor physical access is the baseline expectation for financial institution data centres

requirements.requirement: Configure the access control system to generate real-time alerts for anomalous access patterns including tailgating detection, failed biometric attempts, out-of-hours access, and anti-passback violations
requirements.rationale: Real-time anomaly detection enables immediate investigation of potential physical security breaches rather than discovering them during periodic log reviews

requirements.requirement: Implement automated access provisioning and deprovisioning workflows integrated with the HR system, ensuring access rights are automatically flagged for revocation upon termination or role change events
requirements.rationale: Manual revocation processes are prone to delays and oversights; automated integration with HR systems ensures timely and reliable access removal

governance.summary: Governance must enforce quarterly access reviews with documented evidence, immediate revocation procedures for terminations and role changes, and formal access request and approval workflows.

requirements.requirement: Conduct formal quarterly access reviews of all data centre access rights, comparing the access list against current employee rosters, role requirements, and business justification, with documented sign-off by the approving authority
requirements.rationale: BNM mandates at least quarterly access reviews; documented reviews with sign-off demonstrate active access governance and are a primary audit evidence requirement

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal access request and approval process requiring line manager and data centre manager approval, documented business justification, and defined access expiry for temporary access grants
requirements.rationale: Ad hoc access grants without formal approval create accountability gaps; a structured process ensures every access right has a documented business justification and approval chain

requirements.requirement: Maintain complete access revocation records with timestamps showing time between termination notification and access removal, targeting revocation within 24 hours for all terminations
requirements.rationale: BNM examiners specifically verify revocation timeliness; documented timestamps provide auditable evidence that the institution meets its own revocation SLAs

business.summary: Formally defined and monitored SLAs for critical technology services ensure that business expectations for system availability, performance, and incident response are clearly articulated, measured, and reported to senior management for accountability.

requirements.requirement: Define SLAs for all critical technology services including core banking, internet banking, mobile banking, ATM networks, and payment processing, with specific availability targets (e.g. 99.9%), performance metrics, and incident response times
requirements.rationale: Without formally defined SLAs, there is no measurable standard against which to hold technology teams accountable, and business expectations remain ambiguous

requirements.requirement: Report SLA performance to senior management at least monthly, including availability percentages, performance trends, breach incidents, and corrective actions taken
requirements.rationale: BNM explicitly requires monthly reporting to senior management; absence of regular reporting indicates weak oversight of technology service delivery

requirements.requirement: Establish financial and operational consequences for SLA breaches including penalty clauses in vendor contracts and internal escalation procedures for persistent underperformance
requirements.rationale: SLAs without consequences are aspirational targets rather than enforceable commitments; meaningful consequences drive investment in maintaining service levels

technology.summary: SLA monitoring must be automated through real-time dashboards and alerting systems that continuously track availability, response times, throughput, and error rates against defined thresholds for all critical services.

requirements.requirement: Implement automated SLA monitoring using application performance monitoring (APM) tools, synthetic monitoring, and real-user monitoring to continuously track availability, response times, and throughput against defined targets
requirements.rationale: Manual SLA tracking is unreliable and retrospective; automated monitoring provides real-time visibility and immediate alerting when services approach or breach SLA thresholds

requirements.requirement: Configure automated SLA breach alerting with tiered escalation from operations team (warning threshold) to IT management (breach threshold) to senior management (critical or repeated breaches)
requirements.rationale: Timely escalation of SLA breaches enables rapid response and prevents minor issues from escalating into major service disruptions

requirements.requirement: Generate automated monthly SLA performance reports with trend analysis, breach summaries, root cause analysis of significant breaches, and recommendations for improvement
requirements.rationale: Consistent, automated reporting ensures accuracy and timeliness of the monthly management reports required by BNM

governance.summary: Governance must ensure SLAs are formally documented and approved by both business and technology stakeholders, monitored continuously, reported regularly, and reviewed annually to reflect changing business needs.

requirements.requirement: Maintain formally documented SLAs signed off by both business owners and technology owners, with clear definitions of availability, performance metrics, measurement methods, reporting frequency, and breach escalation procedures
requirements.rationale: Joint sign-off ensures alignment between business expectations and technology capabilities, and provides clear accountability for service delivery

requirements.requirement: Track all SLA breaches in a formal register with root cause analysis, corrective actions, responsible owners, target resolution dates, and verification of effectiveness once remediated
requirements.rationale: A breach register with tracked remediation demonstrates to BNM that the institution learns from failures and takes systematic corrective action

requirements.requirement: Review and update SLAs at least annually in consultation with business stakeholders to reflect changes in business requirements, technology capabilities, and regulatory expectations
requirements.rationale: Static SLAs become misaligned with evolving business needs; annual reviews ensure targets remain relevant and appropriately challenging

business.summary: IT service continuity plans ensure that critical banking services can survive disruptions from natural disasters, cyber attacks, and technology failures, directly protecting the institution's ability to serve customers and meet regulatory obligations during crises.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain IT service continuity plans for all critical technology services that are aligned with the institution's business continuity management (BCM) programme and approved by senior management
requirements.rationale: Misalignment between IT continuity and business continuity creates gaps where critical business processes lack adequate technology recovery support during disruptions

requirements.requirement: Ensure continuity plans address multiple disruption scenarios including natural disasters (floods, earthquakes), cyber attacks (ransomware, DDoS), technology failures (hardware, software, network), and pandemic/workforce unavailability
requirements.rationale: Plans that only address a single scenario type leave the institution unprepared for the full range of threats; BNM expects comprehensive scenario coverage

requirements.requirement: Allocate budget for continuity infrastructure including disaster recovery sites, redundant systems, and regular testing exercises to validate continuity capabilities
requirements.rationale: Continuity plans without funded infrastructure are theoretical documents; real resilience requires investment in redundant capacity and regular testing

technology.summary: IT service continuity plans must define technical recovery procedures, infrastructure requirements, data replication strategies, and failover mechanisms for each critical service, covering natural disasters, cyber attacks, and technology failures.

requirements.requirement: Document detailed technical recovery procedures for each critical system including step-by-step failover processes, data restoration procedures, service validation checks, and rollback plans
requirements.rationale: Generic recovery plans are unusable during a crisis; detailed, tested procedures enable operations teams to execute recovery quickly and accurately under pressure

requirements.requirement: Implement data replication and backup strategies aligned with RPO targets for each critical system, including real-time replication for Tier 1 systems and daily backups with off-site storage for lower tiers
requirements.rationale: Data replication strategies must match the RPO requirements defined by business impact analysis; misalignment means data loss during failover may exceed business tolerance

requirements.requirement: Test IT service continuity plans at least annually for all critical systems, including tabletop exercises, partial failover tests, and full failover tests on a rotating schedule
requirements.rationale: Untested plans provide false confidence; regular testing across multiple exercise types validates both technical procedures and team readiness

requirements.requirement: Maintain an up-to-date technology dependency map linking each critical business process to its supporting technology services, infrastructure components, and third-party dependencies
requirements.rationale: Recovery planning requires understanding of service dependencies; without a current dependency map, recovery teams may overlook critical upstream or downstream dependencies

governance.summary: Governance must ensure continuity plans are formally documented, aligned with BCM, regularly tested, and subject to post-test reviews with deficiency tracking and remediation.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal alignment mapping between IT service continuity plans and the institution's business continuity plans, demonstrating that every critical business process has corresponding IT recovery coverage
requirements.rationale: BNM examiners verify alignment between BCM and IT continuity; a documented mapping provides clear evidence of coordination between business and technology planning

requirements.requirement: Conduct post-test reviews after every continuity exercise, documenting lessons learned, identified deficiencies, and corrective actions with assigned owners and target completion dates
requirements.rationale: Testing without structured follow-up wastes the learning opportunity; tracked deficiency remediation demonstrates continuous improvement in continuity capability

requirements.requirement: Report IT service continuity status including plan currency, test results, and outstanding deficiencies to the Board Risk Committee at least semi-annually
requirements.rationale: Board-level visibility ensures that continuity planning receives adequate attention and resources, and demonstrates governance oversight to BNM

business.summary: Comprehensive disaster recovery plans for every critical system ensure the institution can restore banking services within acceptable timeframes following catastrophic failures, protecting customers, shareholders, and the broader financial system.

requirements.requirement: Ensure every critical system has a documented disaster recovery plan that defines recovery procedures, roles and responsibilities, communication plans, and escalation procedures, approved by the relevant business owner
requirements.rationale: Systems without DR plans represent single points of failure for the business; BNM expects complete DR coverage for all critical systems, not just a subset

requirements.requirement: Ensure DR plans are accessible to relevant personnel during emergencies via out-of-band channels (printed copies, offline devices, secondary cloud storage) that do not depend on the systems being recovered
requirements.rationale: DR plans stored only on the systems they are designed to recover are useless during a disaster; out-of-band accessibility is a common BNM finding

requirements.requirement: Define clear communication protocols for DR activation including notification trees for internal stakeholders, regulators, customers, and media, with predefined templates and spokesperson assignments
requirements.rationale: Poor communication during a disaster extends recovery time, causes reputational damage, and may breach regulatory notification requirements

technology.summary: DR plans must contain detailed, step-by-step recovery procedures for each critical system, including infrastructure rebuild, data restoration, application recovery, service validation, and failback procedures.

requirements.requirement: Document step-by-step recovery procedures for each critical system including prerequisites, infrastructure provisioning, data restoration from backups or replication, application deployment, configuration validation, and service acceptance testing
requirements.rationale: Generic or high-level procedures are unusable under the stress of an actual disaster; detailed, tested steps enable recovery by any qualified team member

requirements.requirement: Maintain a DR roles and responsibilities matrix (RACI) for each critical system, identifying the DR coordinator, technical recovery leads, business validation owners, communication leads, and vendor contacts
requirements.rationale: Confusion over roles during a disaster causes delays and duplicated effort; a predefined RACI ensures everyone knows their responsibilities before an incident occurs

requirements.requirement: Include failback procedures in all DR plans covering the safe return of services from the DR site to the primary site, including data synchronisation, cutover scheduling, and validation testing
requirements.rationale: Many DR plans focus only on failover; failback without proper procedures can cause data loss or extended dual-running, increasing costs and complexity

requirements.requirement: Define escalation procedures with time-based triggers: if recovery is not progressing within defined milestones, escalation to senior management and consideration of alternate recovery strategies must be automatic
requirements.rationale: Recovery efforts that stall without escalation consume critical time; predefined escalation triggers ensure timely management intervention when recovery deviates from plan

governance.summary: Governance must ensure DR plans are formally documented, version-controlled, reviewed at least annually, and distributed to all relevant personnel with confirmation of receipt and understanding.

requirements.requirement: Maintain all DR plans under formal version control with annual review cycles, change tracking, and documented approval by both technology and business owners
requirements.rationale: Outdated DR plans that do not reflect current infrastructure and application configurations will fail during execution; version control ensures currency and auditability

requirements.requirement: Distribute updated DR plans to all named personnel in the RACI matrix and maintain confirmation records that each individual has received, read, and understood their role
requirements.rationale: Plans are only effective if the people responsible for executing them are familiar with the content; distribution confirmation provides evidence of readiness

requirements.requirement: Report DR plan coverage, currency, and readiness status to the Board Risk Committee at least semi-annually, highlighting any critical systems without current DR plans
requirements.rationale: Board visibility ensures DR planning receives adequate priority and resources, and gaps are addressed before they materialise during an actual disaster

business.summary: Annual DR testing with full failover validates that recovery plans actually work under realistic conditions, confirming that RTO and RPO targets are achievable and identifying deficiencies before a real disaster occurs.

requirements.requirement: Conduct full failover DR tests at least annually for all critical systems, transferring operations to the DR site and validating that business transactions can be processed normally from the recovery environment
requirements.rationale: BNM mandates annual full failover testing; tabletop exercises alone are insufficient to validate that recovery procedures actually work in practice

requirements.requirement: Involve business users in DR testing to validate that recovered systems support actual business operations, not just technical availability, including transaction processing, reporting, and customer-facing functionality
requirements.rationale: A system that is technically recovered but functionally broken provides false assurance; business validation confirms that recovery meets operational requirements

requirements.requirement: Track and remediate all deficiencies identified during DR testing within defined timelines, with progress reported to the IT Risk Committee until resolution is confirmed
requirements.rationale: DR test deficiencies that are not remediated accumulate and compound, progressively degrading the institution's recovery capability

technology.summary: DR testing must include full failover to the DR site, validation of RTO and RPO achievement, end-to-end functional testing of recovered services, and documented remediation of any deficiencies identified.

requirements.requirement: Execute full failover tests that transfer all critical system operations to the DR site, measuring actual recovery times against defined RTOs and verifying data integrity against defined RPOs
requirements.rationale: Partial or simulated tests do not validate the complete recovery chain; full failover is the only way to confirm that RTO and RPO targets are actually achievable

requirements.requirement: Document detailed test results for each critical system including planned vs actual recovery times, data loss measurements, functional test outcomes, and any issues encountered during the failover and failback process
requirements.rationale: Detailed documentation enables gap analysis, trend tracking across test cycles, and provides comprehensive evidence for BNM examination

requirements.requirement: Develop and track a deficiency remediation plan for every issue identified during DR testing, with risk-prioritised timelines, assigned owners, and verification testing to confirm remediation effectiveness
requirements.rationale: Identified deficiencies without tracked remediation demonstrate inadequate follow-through; BNM examiners will verify that test findings are systematically addressed

requirements.requirement: Rotate DR test scenarios annually to cover different failure modes including complete site loss, partial infrastructure failure, cyber attack scenarios, and cascading failures across dependent systems
requirements.rationale: Repeating the same test scenario every year provides diminishing returns; varied scenarios test the institution's ability to respond to different types of disruptions

governance.summary: Governance must ensure DR testing is scheduled, results are formally documented and reviewed by management, deficiencies are tracked to closure, and testing coverage is reported to the board.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal annual DR test schedule covering all critical systems, approved by the CTO, with defined test objectives, success criteria, and participant lists
requirements.rationale: An approved schedule ensures DR testing is planned and resourced rather than ad hoc, and provides a framework for measuring test coverage and completeness

requirements.requirement: Present DR test results and deficiency remediation status to the IT Risk Committee within 30 days of each test completion, with executive summary highlighting pass/fail outcomes and critical findings
requirements.rationale: Timely management reporting ensures DR test outcomes receive appropriate attention and resources for remediation before institutional memory fades

requirements.requirement: Report annual DR testing coverage, pass rates, and outstanding deficiencies to the Board Risk Committee as part of the technology resilience reporting cycle
requirements.rationale: Board-level reporting on DR test outcomes provides governance oversight and ensures the institution's recovery capabilities are understood at the highest level

business.summary: Formally defined and business-owner-approved RTO and RPO targets ensure that recovery priorities are driven by business impact rather than technical convenience, and that DR investments are proportionate to the criticality of each system.

requirements.requirement: Define RTO and RPO targets for all critical systems based on business impact analysis results, with formal approval from the business owner of each system documenting acceptance of the defined recovery targets
requirements.rationale: Technology teams cannot unilaterally set recovery targets; business owners must accept the residual risk of the defined RTO/RPO based on their understanding of business impact

requirements.requirement: Ensure RTO and RPO targets reflect the maximum tolerable downtime and data loss for each critical business process, informed by financial impact analysis, regulatory requirements, and customer expectations
requirements.rationale: RTO/RPO targets that are not grounded in business impact analysis may be either too aggressive (wasting resources) or too lenient (accepting unacceptable business risk)

requirements.requirement: Review RTO and RPO targets annually with business owners to ensure they remain aligned with evolving business requirements, regulatory changes, and customer expectations
requirements.rationale: Business criticality changes over time as new products launch, regulatory requirements evolve, and customer expectations increase; static targets become misaligned

technology.summary: RTO and RPO targets must be technically achievable with current infrastructure, validated through regular DR testing, and supported by appropriate replication, backup, and recovery mechanisms for each tier of criticality.

requirements.requirement: Map each critical system's RTO and RPO targets to the technical capabilities required to achieve them, including replication technology, backup frequency, recovery automation, and DR site capacity
requirements.rationale: RTO/RPO targets that exceed technical capabilities are aspirational rather than achievable; mapping ensures infrastructure investment aligns with recovery commitments

requirements.requirement: Validate RTO and RPO achievement through annual DR testing, measuring actual recovery times and data loss against defined targets, and documenting any variances
requirements.rationale: BNM requires validated RTO/RPO; unvalidated targets provide false assurance about the institution's actual recovery capability

requirements.requirement: Implement tiered recovery strategies aligned with system criticality: Tier 1 (real-time replication, RTO under 2 hours), Tier 2 (near-real-time replication, RTO under 8 hours), and Tier 3 (daily backup restoration, RTO under 24 hours)
requirements.rationale: A one-size-fits-all approach is either too expensive or inadequate; tiered strategies optimise investment while ensuring critical systems receive the fastest recovery

governance.summary: Governance must ensure RTO/RPO targets are formally documented with business owner sign-off, linked to BIA results, validated through testing, and reported to management with any gaps between targets and actual capabilities.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal register of all critical systems with their defined RTO and RPO targets, business owner approval evidence, BIA reference, and latest DR test validation results
requirements.rationale: A centralised register provides a single source of truth for recovery targets and their validation status, essential for both management oversight and BNM examination

requirements.requirement: Report RTO/RPO validation results to the IT Risk Committee after each DR test cycle, highlighting systems that failed to meet their targets and the remediation plans to close the gaps
requirements.rationale: Gaps between defined targets and actual capabilities represent unmitigated business risk that requires management decision-making on additional investment or risk acceptance

requirements.requirement: Ensure that any changes to RTO/RPO targets require formal change approval from business owners, with documented justification and impact assessment on DR infrastructure requirements
requirements.rationale: Informal changes to recovery targets can misalign business expectations with technology capabilities; formal change control maintains accountability and traceability

business.summary: An annual business impact analysis identifies which business processes are critical, quantifies the impact of disruption, and provides the foundation for recovery prioritisation, resource allocation, and continuity planning decisions across the institution.

requirements.requirement: Conduct a comprehensive business impact analysis at least annually, covering all business processes, their supporting technology services, and the financial, operational, regulatory, and reputational impact of disruption at various time intervals
requirements.rationale: BNM mandates annual BIA; without it, the institution lacks an objective basis for recovery prioritisation and may invest in protecting the wrong systems

requirements.requirement: Engage business process owners across all divisions (retail banking, corporate banking, treasury, operations, compliance) in the BIA process to ensure accurate impact assessments and buy-in for recovery priorities
requirements.rationale: IT-driven BIAs without business involvement produce inaccurate impact assessments; business owners understand the true operational and financial consequences of disruption

requirements.requirement: Use BIA results to inform resource allocation for continuity and recovery investments, ensuring the highest-impact processes receive proportionate protection and recovery capabilities
requirements.rationale: Without BIA-driven resource allocation, investment decisions are made on an ad hoc basis, potentially leaving the most critical processes underprotected

technology.summary: The BIA must map all critical business processes to their supporting technology services, infrastructure components, and third-party dependencies, identifying single points of failure and informing technology recovery priorities.

requirements.requirement: Map every critical business process to its supporting technology services, underlying infrastructure components (servers, databases, networks), and third-party dependencies (cloud services, payment networks, data feeds)
requirements.rationale: Technology dependency mapping is essential for identifying single points of failure and ensuring DR plans cover the complete technology stack supporting each critical process

requirements.requirement: Identify and document single points of failure for each critical technology service, assessing the impact of failure and recommending redundancy or mitigation measures
requirements.rationale: Single points of failure represent the weakest links in the technology chain; the BIA must highlight them so they can be addressed through redundancy or compensating controls

requirements.requirement: Maintain an up-to-date critical business process inventory with assigned criticality tiers (Tier 1: mission-critical, Tier 2: important, Tier 3: standard) based on BIA results, driving differentiated recovery strategies
requirements.rationale: Tiered classification enables proportionate investment in recovery capabilities; treating all systems equally is either wastefully expensive or inadequately protective

governance.summary: Governance must ensure the BIA is conducted annually with broad stakeholder engagement, results are formally documented and approved, and BIA findings drive tangible actions in continuity planning and resource allocation.

requirements.requirement: Present BIA findings and recommendations to the Board Risk Committee, including the critical process inventory, identified gaps, and proposed investment priorities for continuity and recovery
requirements.rationale: Board-level review of BIA findings ensures that recovery priorities are endorsed at the highest governance level and that investment requests are supported by objective analysis

requirements.requirement: Maintain formal BIA documentation with version control, including methodology, stakeholder input records, impact assessments, and a clear linkage from BIA results to RTO/RPO targets and continuity plan priorities
requirements.rationale: Comprehensive BIA documentation demonstrates to BNM examiners that the analysis is rigorous, evidence-based, and directly informs the institution's continuity planning

requirements.requirement: Track implementation of BIA recommendations through a formal action register with assigned owners, target dates, and completion evidence, reporting progress to the IT Risk Committee quarterly
requirements.rationale: BIA findings that do not translate into action represent wasted effort; tracked recommendations with management reporting ensure accountability for follow-through

business.summary: Effective crisis communication during technology incidents protects the institution's reputation, maintains customer confidence, ensures timely regulatory notification, and coordinates stakeholder response to minimise the overall impact of disruptions.

requirements.requirement: Establish a crisis communication plan covering internal stakeholder notification (board, senior management, staff), external stakeholder notification (BNM, customers, payment networks), and media management during technology incidents and disruptions
requirements.rationale: Poor communication during a crisis causes reputational damage disproportionate to the actual incident; proactive, structured communication maintains stakeholder confidence

requirements.requirement: Define stakeholder notification timelines aligned with regulatory requirements (BNM incident reporting within prescribed timeframes) and contractual obligations (payment network notification requirements)
requirements.rationale: Late or missing regulatory notifications can result in additional regulatory action beyond the original incident; predefined timelines ensure compliance

requirements.requirement: Designate trained spokespersons for crisis communication with customers and media, with pre-approved message templates for common incident types (service outage, data breach, cyber attack)
requirements.rationale: Ad hoc communication by untrained personnel during a crisis can cause further reputational damage; designated spokespersons with prepared messaging ensure consistent and appropriate communication

technology.summary: Crisis communication infrastructure must include redundant communication channels (out-of-band from affected systems), automated notification systems, and secure collaboration platforms that remain operational during technology disruptions.

requirements.requirement: Implement out-of-band communication channels for crisis coordination that do not depend on the institution's primary technology infrastructure, including satellite phones, mobile messaging platforms, and backup email services
requirements.rationale: Communication systems that rely on the same infrastructure being recovered are useless during major disruptions; out-of-band channels ensure coordination continues regardless of the incident scope

requirements.requirement: Deploy automated mass notification systems capable of sending alerts to predefined stakeholder groups via multiple channels (SMS, email, voice call, mobile app push notification) with delivery confirmation tracking
requirements.rationale: Manual notification of large stakeholder groups is slow and error-prone; automated systems ensure rapid, comprehensive notification with auditable delivery confirmation

requirements.requirement: Maintain up-to-date stakeholder contact lists with primary and alternate contact details for all crisis communication recipients, validated at least quarterly
requirements.rationale: Outdated contact lists are the most common reason crisis notifications fail to reach intended recipients; quarterly validation ensures list accuracy

governance.summary: Governance must ensure crisis communication plans are formally documented, regularly tested as part of continuity exercises, and reviewed after every significant incident to incorporate lessons learned.

requirements.requirement: Document the crisis communication plan with clear protocols defining when to activate, who makes the activation decision, notification sequences for each stakeholder group, and templates for different incident types
requirements.rationale: A documented plan with clear activation criteria prevents hesitation and confusion during crises, and provides auditable evidence of communication preparedness for BNM

requirements.requirement: Test crisis communication plans as part of regular continuity exercises at least annually, validating notification speed, stakeholder reachability, and effectiveness of communication channels
requirements.rationale: BNM expects communication plans to be tested alongside continuity plans; untested communication procedures are likely to fail under real crisis conditions

requirements.requirement: Conduct post-incident reviews of crisis communication effectiveness after every significant technology incident, documenting lessons learned and updating plans and templates accordingly
requirements.rationale: Real incidents provide the best learning opportunities for improving crisis communication; structured post-incident reviews capture these lessons systematically

business.summary: A well-designed and documented network architecture with defence-in-depth principles is the foundation of the institution's cyber resilience, protecting all banking channels and customer data from network-based attacks.

requirements.requirement: Invest in a secure network architecture designed with defence-in-depth principles, including multiple security layers, network segmentation, and redundant connectivity for all critical banking services
requirements.rationale: A flat or poorly designed network allows attackers to move laterally after initial compromise; defence-in-depth architecture limits the blast radius of security incidents

requirements.requirement: Ensure complete and current network architecture documentation is maintained, including all network zones, data flows, interconnections, and trust boundaries, reviewed at least annually
requirements.rationale: Undocumented network architecture makes it impossible to conduct effective security reviews, troubleshoot incidents, or demonstrate compliance to BNM examiners

requirements.requirement: Conduct network architecture reviews upon any significant infrastructure changes (cloud migrations, new partner connections, mergers) in addition to the mandatory annual review
requirements.rationale: Significant changes can introduce architectural weaknesses; reviewing the architecture before and after major changes ensures security is maintained throughout transitions

technology.summary: The network architecture must implement defence-in-depth with clearly defined security zones (DMZ, internal, restricted, management), documented data flows, controlled interconnections, and multiple layers of security controls at each boundary.

requirements.requirement: Design and maintain a network architecture with clearly defined security zones including DMZ, internal network, restricted/sensitive data zones, management network, and guest/visitor network, each with appropriate boundary controls
requirements.rationale: Clearly defined zones with controlled boundaries ensure that traffic flows through security inspection points and that sensitive systems are isolated from less trusted areas

requirements.requirement: Maintain comprehensive network architecture diagrams including logical topology, physical topology, data flow diagrams, security zone boundaries, firewall placement, and all external interconnections (internet, SWIFT, payment networks, partner connections)
requirements.rationale: Complete diagrams are essential for security reviews, incident response, and BNM examination; outdated or incomplete diagrams indicate poor network governance

requirements.requirement: Implement defence-in-depth controls at each network layer including perimeter firewalls, internal firewalls between zones, intrusion detection/prevention systems, web application firewalls, and network access control
requirements.rationale: No single security control is infallible; layered defences ensure that failure or bypass of one control does not grant unrestricted access to the network

requirements.requirement: Conduct formal network architecture reviews at least annually and upon significant changes, assessing security zone adequacy, control effectiveness, and alignment with current threat intelligence
requirements.rationale: Network architectures that are not regularly reviewed drift from security best practices as new threats emerge and business requirements evolve

governance.summary: Governance must ensure the network architecture is formally documented and approved, subject to regular independent reviews, and changes are managed through a formal architecture change process.

requirements.requirement: Maintain the network architecture documentation as a formally controlled document with version history, approval by the CISO or CTO, and mandatory review upon significant changes and at least annually
requirements.rationale: Formally controlled architecture documentation provides a reliable baseline for security assessments and demonstrates to BNM that network design is actively governed

requirements.requirement: Require all significant network architecture changes to go through a formal security review and approval process before implementation, with documented risk assessment and sign-off
requirements.rationale: Unreviewed network changes can introduce security weaknesses; a formal review process ensures security implications are assessed and approved before deployment

requirements.requirement: Commission independent network architecture assessments at least annually, conducted by qualified network security professionals, with findings reported to the IT Risk Committee
requirements.rationale: Internal reviews may miss design weaknesses due to familiarity bias; independent assessments provide objective validation of the architecture's security posture

business.summary: Network segmentation isolates critical systems and sensitive data environments from the broader network, limiting the impact of security breaches by preventing attackers from moving laterally to reach the institution's most valuable assets.

requirements.requirement: Implement network segmentation that isolates critical systems (core banking, payment processing), sensitive data environments (customer databases, card data), and different trust zones (production, development, management) from each other
requirements.rationale: Flat networks allow an attacker who compromises any system to reach all systems; segmentation ensures that a breach in one area cannot spread to critical banking operations

requirements.requirement: Validate network segmentation effectiveness through regular penetration testing that specifically includes segmentation bypass testing to confirm that lateral movement between zones is effectively prevented
requirements.rationale: Segmentation that exists on paper but can be bypassed provides false security; penetration testing validates that controls actually prevent the lateral movement they are designed to stop

requirements.requirement: Ensure PCI DSS cardholder data environments are segmented from all other networks with validated segmentation controls, supporting the institution's PCI compliance obligations
requirements.rationale: PCI DSS requires validated network segmentation for cardholder data environments; failure to segment expands the PCI scope to the entire network and increases compliance costs

technology.summary: Network segmentation must be implemented through VLANs, firewalls, micro-segmentation, and access control lists, with traffic between zones restricted to explicitly permitted flows and validated through penetration testing.

requirements.requirement: Implement network segmentation using VLANs, firewall rules, and access control lists to create isolated zones for critical systems, sensitive data, development/test, management, and guest networks, with inter-zone traffic restricted to explicitly defined and justified flows
requirements.rationale: Effective segmentation requires both logical separation (VLANs) and policy enforcement (firewalls/ACLs); either alone is insufficient to prevent lateral movement

requirements.requirement: Document the segmentation design including zone definitions, trust levels, permitted inter-zone flows with business justification, and the technical controls enforcing each boundary
requirements.rationale: Undocumented segmentation is difficult to maintain consistently and impossible to audit; documentation provides the baseline for reviews and penetration testing scope

requirements.requirement: Include network segmentation validation as a specific scope item in annual penetration testing, with testers attempting lateral movement between zones and documenting any successful boundary crossings
requirements.rationale: BNM requires segmentation controls to be validated through penetration testing; standard penetration tests may not specifically test segmentation unless explicitly scoped

requirements.requirement: Consider implementing micro-segmentation for the most sensitive environments (cardholder data, core banking databases) to provide granular, workload-level isolation beyond traditional network segmentation
requirements.rationale: Traditional VLAN-based segmentation provides zone-level isolation; micro-segmentation adds workload-level controls that further limit lateral movement within sensitive zones

governance.summary: Governance must ensure segmentation design is formally approved, penetration test results are reviewed and deficiencies remediated, and segmentation controls are subject to regular independent validation.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal network segmentation policy defining zone classifications, trust levels, inter-zone traffic rules, and the approval process for new inter-zone connections, approved by the CISO
requirements.rationale: A formal policy ensures segmentation decisions are consistent, justified, and traceable; ad hoc inter-zone connections can erode segmentation effectiveness over time

requirements.requirement: Review penetration testing results specifically for segmentation findings, tracking any successful boundary crossings as high-priority deficiencies with mandatory remediation timelines
requirements.rationale: A successful segmentation bypass in a penetration test represents a critical finding that could allow real attackers to reach sensitive systems; immediate remediation is essential

requirements.requirement: Conduct semi-annual reviews of inter-zone firewall rules to identify and remove any unnecessary, expired, or overly permissive rules that could weaken segmentation boundaries
requirements.rationale: Firewall rules accumulate over time; without regular reviews, temporary or overly broad rules create unintended pathways that undermine the segmentation architecture

business.summary: Firewalls are the primary enforcement mechanism for network security policy; poorly managed firewall rules with permissive or stale entries create direct pathways for attackers to reach critical banking systems and sensitive customer data.

requirements.requirement: Deploy and maintain firewalls at all network boundaries (internet, partner connections, payment networks) and between all internal security zones, enforcing the principle of least privilege for all traffic flows
requirements.rationale: Network boundaries without firewall protection expose internal systems directly to external threats; BNM expects firewall coverage at every boundary and between zones

requirements.requirement: Conduct firewall rule reviews at least semi-annually to identify and remediate unused, redundant, overly permissive, or shadow rules that weaken the institution's network security posture
requirements.rationale: BNM mandates at least semi-annual rule reviews; firewall rules accumulate over time and without regular cleanup, the rule base becomes bloated and permissive

requirements.requirement: Ensure all firewall rule changes go through a formal change management process with security review, business justification, defined expiry for temporary rules, and approval before implementation
requirements.rationale: Uncontrolled firewall changes are a leading cause of security incidents; formal change management ensures every rule has a documented purpose and approval

technology.summary: Firewall management must include least-privilege rule design, automated rule analysis tools, semi-annual rule reviews, centralised management platforms, and documented change management processes for all rule modifications.

requirements.requirement: Implement a default-deny firewall policy at all boundaries, with explicit allow rules only for documented, justified, and approved traffic flows, using specific source/destination addresses and ports rather than 'any' entries
requirements.rationale: Default-deny ensures only explicitly permitted traffic passes; 'any' rules in source, destination, or port fields create overly permissive pathways that undermine network security

requirements.requirement: Deploy firewall rule analysis tools (e.g. Tufin, AlgoSec, FireMon) to automatically identify unused rules, shadowed rules, overly permissive rules, and compliance violations, supporting the semi-annual review process
requirements.rationale: Manual analysis of large rule bases is impractical and error-prone; automated tools identify issues that would be missed in manual reviews and accelerate the review cycle

requirements.requirement: Implement centralised firewall management across all firewalls in the estate, with consistent rule naming conventions, standardised logging, and unified change tracking
requirements.rationale: Decentralised management leads to inconsistent rule application and makes comprehensive auditing difficult; centralisation ensures consistency and simplifies compliance monitoring

requirements.requirement: Configure comprehensive firewall logging with all permitted and denied traffic logged, log retention for a minimum of 12 months, and integration with the SIEM platform for real-time analysis
requirements.rationale: Firewall logs are essential for incident investigation, forensics, and compliance; without comprehensive logging, security events may go undetected or be uninvestigable

governance.summary: Governance must enforce a formal firewall management policy, ensure semi-annual rule reviews are conducted and documented, and track remediation of identified rule base deficiencies.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal firewall management policy covering rule design principles (default-deny, least privilege), change management procedures, review frequency (minimum semi-annual), and rule lifecycle management including expiry of temporary rules
requirements.rationale: A comprehensive policy provides the governance framework for consistent firewall management and is a primary document BNM examiners will request during examination

requirements.requirement: Document all semi-annual firewall rule reviews with findings, remediation actions, rule count trends, and percentage of rules with documented business justification, reporting results to the IT Risk Committee
requirements.rationale: Documented reviews with metrics demonstrate to BNM that the institution actively manages its firewall rule base rather than allowing unchecked rule accumulation

requirements.requirement: Maintain an audit trail of all firewall rule changes including the requester, business justification, security review outcome, approver, implementation date, and planned expiry date for temporary rules
requirements.rationale: Complete change audit trails are essential for forensic investigation, compliance demonstration, and accountability for firewall rule changes

business.summary: Failure to deploy and maintain IDS/IPS at critical network points exposes the institution to undetected intrusions that could compromise customer data, disrupt banking services, and result in significant financial and reputational losses.

requirements.requirement: Define and maintain a register of critical network points where IDS/IPS must be deployed, aligned with the institution's network architecture and threat landscape
requirements.rationale: Ensures IDS/IPS coverage is comprehensive and no critical network segment is left unmonitored

requirements.requirement: Establish escalation procedures for IDS/IPS alerts that ensure timely investigation and response to detected threats
requirements.rationale: Alerts without investigation provide no security value and create a false sense of protection

requirements.requirement: Allocate budget and resources for IDS/IPS signature subscription renewals, hardware lifecycle replacement, and SOC staffing to monitor alerts
requirements.rationale: Underfunded IDS/IPS programmes degrade rapidly, leaving the institution exposed to evolving threats

technology.summary: IDS/IPS systems must be deployed inline or via network taps at critical points including perimeter, DMZ, and inter-zone boundaries, with automated signature updates and integration into the SIEM for centralised alert correlation.

requirements.requirement: Deploy IDS/IPS sensors at all critical network points including internet gateways, DMZ boundaries, core banking network segments, and inter-VLAN transit points
requirements.rationale: Comprehensive sensor placement ensures malicious traffic is detected regardless of entry point or lateral movement

requirements.requirement: Configure automated IDS/IPS signature and rule updates with a maximum delay of 24 hours from vendor release, and validate updates do not disrupt legitimate traffic
requirements.rationale: Outdated signatures leave the institution vulnerable to newly discovered attack vectors

requirements.requirement: Integrate IDS/IPS alerts into the centralised SIEM platform with correlation rules to identify attack patterns and reduce false positives
requirements.rationale: Centralised correlation enables faster detection of coordinated attacks and reduces alert fatigue

requirements.requirement: Tune IDS/IPS rules to minimise false positives while maintaining detection efficacy, with documented tuning rationale for each suppressed or modified rule
requirements.rationale: Excessive false positives lead to alert fatigue and missed genuine threats

governance.summary: A formal IDS/IPS policy must define deployment standards, signature update schedules, alert investigation SLAs, and periodic effectiveness reviews to demonstrate ongoing compliance.

requirements.requirement: Establish and maintain an IDS/IPS policy defining deployment standards, signature update frequency, alert triage SLAs, and escalation procedures
requirements.rationale: Formalised policy ensures consistent deployment and operation across all network segments

requirements.requirement: Conduct quarterly reviews of IDS/IPS effectiveness including detection rates, false positive ratios, mean time to investigate, and coverage gaps
requirements.rationale: Regular effectiveness reviews ensure the IDS/IPS programme remains fit for purpose as threats evolve

requirements.requirement: Maintain documented records of all IDS/IPS alert investigations including triage decisions, investigation findings, and remediation actions taken
requirements.rationale: Investigation records provide audit trail evidence and support continuous improvement of detection capabilities

business.summary: Unsecured wireless networks provide an easily exploitable attack vector that can bypass perimeter defences, potentially exposing sensitive banking data and internal systems to unauthorised access.

requirements.requirement: Establish a wireless network usage policy that defines approved use cases, prohibited activities, and security requirements for all corporate and guest wireless networks
requirements.rationale: Clear policy boundaries prevent ad-hoc wireless deployments that introduce uncontrolled security risks

requirements.requirement: Conduct regular wireless security risk assessments to identify vulnerabilities and ensure controls remain effective against evolving wireless threats
requirements.rationale: Wireless attack techniques evolve rapidly and periodic assessments ensure defences remain current

requirements.requirement: Maintain an inventory of all authorised wireless access points and ensure only approved devices are connected to the corporate network
requirements.rationale: Unknown access points create unmonitored entry points into the corporate network

technology.summary: Wireless networks must enforce WPA3 encryption, be segmented from the corporate LAN, deploy rogue access point detection, and ensure guest networks are fully isolated from internal systems.

requirements.requirement: Configure all corporate wireless networks with WPA3 Enterprise encryption (or WPA2 Enterprise with AES as a transitional minimum) and disable legacy protocols such as WEP and TKIP
requirements.rationale: WPA3 provides the strongest available wireless encryption and protects against offline dictionary attacks

requirements.requirement: Implement network segmentation to isolate wireless networks from the corporate wired network using VLANs and firewall rules, with guest wireless on a completely separate network segment
requirements.rationale: Segmentation prevents wireless network compromises from providing direct access to internal banking systems

requirements.requirement: Deploy a wireless intrusion detection and prevention system (WIDS/WIPS) capable of detecting rogue access points, evil twin attacks, and deauthentication attacks
requirements.rationale: Rogue access points can be used by attackers to intercept traffic or gain unauthorised network access

requirements.requirement: Conduct wireless penetration testing and security assessments at least semi-annually covering all office locations and branch premises
requirements.rationale: Regular testing validates the effectiveness of wireless security controls and identifies configuration weaknesses

governance.summary: A wireless security policy must define encryption standards, segmentation requirements, rogue AP detection procedures, and regular assessment schedules with documented results and remediation tracking.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal wireless security policy covering encryption standards, network segmentation, rogue AP detection, guest network isolation, and assessment schedules
requirements.rationale: A comprehensive policy ensures consistent wireless security controls across all locations

requirements.requirement: Document and track remediation of all findings from wireless security assessments with defined timelines and responsible owners
requirements.rationale: Unresolved wireless security findings represent ongoing vulnerabilities that could be exploited

requirements.requirement: Maintain records of all rogue access point detections, investigations, and removal actions with timestamps and responsible personnel
requirements.rationale: Documentation demonstrates active monitoring and response to wireless threats

business.summary: Insecure remote access exposes the institution to credential theft, man-in-the-middle attacks, and unauthorised entry into corporate systems, particularly in hybrid working arrangements prevalent across Malaysian financial institutions.

requirements.requirement: Define a remote access policy specifying who may access corporate resources remotely, under what conditions, and with what security controls
requirements.rationale: Clear policy boundaries prevent uncontrolled remote access that bypasses the institution's security perimeter

requirements.requirement: Ensure all remote access users complete security awareness training covering phishing risks, secure device handling, and reporting procedures specific to remote working
requirements.rationale: Remote workers face elevated social engineering risks and need targeted security guidance

requirements.requirement: Establish monitoring and reporting mechanisms for remote access usage patterns to detect anomalous behaviour or policy violations
requirements.rationale: Remote access channels are high-value targets for attackers and require continuous oversight

technology.summary: Remote access must be secured with MFA, encrypted VPN tunnels, and endpoint compliance checks, with split tunnelling disabled and all sessions logged and monitored in real time.

requirements.requirement: Implement multi-factor authentication for all remote access connections using hardware tokens or authenticator applications, prohibiting SMS-based OTP
requirements.rationale: MFA prevents compromised credentials from being used to establish remote sessions

requirements.requirement: Deploy encrypted VPN tunnels using IPSec or TLS-based protocols with a minimum of AES-256 encryption for all remote access to the corporate network
requirements.rationale: Encrypted tunnels protect data in transit from interception on untrusted networks

requirements.requirement: Implement endpoint compliance checks (NAC) that verify device health including OS patch level, antivirus status, and disk encryption before granting remote access
requirements.rationale: Unpatched or compromised endpoints connecting remotely can introduce malware into the corporate network

requirements.requirement: Disable split tunnelling on all VPN configurations to ensure all traffic from remote devices traverses the corporate security stack
requirements.rationale: Split tunnelling allows direct internet access that bypasses corporate security controls including web filtering and DLP

governance.summary: Remote access governance requires a formal policy, session logging with retention, periodic access reviews, and documented procedures for granting and revoking remote access privileges.

requirements.requirement: Maintain a formal remote access security policy covering MFA requirements, VPN standards, endpoint compliance, split tunnelling prohibition, and session monitoring
requirements.rationale: A comprehensive policy ensures consistent security controls for all remote access methods and users

requirements.requirement: Log all remote access sessions including connection timestamps, user identity, source IP, duration, and endpoint compliance status, with logs retained for a minimum of three years
requirements.rationale: Session logs are essential for security investigations, compliance audits, and detecting unauthorised access

requirements.requirement: Conduct quarterly reviews of remote access user lists to ensure only authorised personnel retain remote access privileges
requirements.rationale: Regular reviews prevent access creep and ensure terminated or transferred staff no longer have remote access

business.summary: DNS infrastructure is foundational to all banking services; a compromised or unreliable DNS can redirect customers to fraudulent sites, enable data exfiltration via DNS tunnelling, or cause complete service outages.
